title: Getting random 403 Forbidden errors on the dashboard
body: I keep getting 403 errors when I try to access the TaskFlow dashboard. It's not every time — sometimes it loads fine, sometimes I get a 403 page. It seems totally random. My permissions haven't changed and other people on my team say they're seeing it too. Started happening a couple days ago.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — intermittent 403s across multiple team members definitely sounds like something changed on the backend rather than a permissions issue on your end.

To start narrowing this down: **Were there any deployments, infrastructure changes, or configuration updates to TaskFlow in the past few days** (around when this started)? And when you hit the 403, can you share the **exact response body** — is it a TaskFlow error page, a generic server 403, or something from a proxy/CDN (e.g., Cloudflare, nginx)? If you can check the browser Network tab on a failed request and grab the response headers and body, that would be very helpful.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah, I think there was a deployment a couple days ago — they added a new role or something. I was actually just assigned the 'analyst' role around that time. As for the error page, I'm not 100% sure but it looks like a pretty standard 403 page, not a custom TaskFlow one. I haven't really dug into the network tab but I can try next time it happens. I always assumed it was network issues since it seems to work if I just refresh a couple times.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really useful detail about the deployment and the new 'analyst' role — that timing lines up suspiciously well.

Quick but important question: **do the other team members who are also seeing the 403 errors all have the 'analyst' role too?** And conversely, are there people on your team who *don't* have that role and are *not* seeing these errors?

This will help us figure out whether the 403s are tied to that role's permission configuration or something broader.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Hmm, good question. I think the others who mentioned it are also analysts — we were all moved to that role around the same time. I haven't heard complaints from the folks who are admins or editors, but I'm not 100% sure if they're just not saying anything or if they're actually fine. Let me ask around and get back to you on that.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for checking on that — the analyst-role correlation will be really helpful to confirm.

In the meantime, I want to dig into the intermittent nature of this, because a simple permissions misconfiguration would fail *every* time, not randomly. A few specifics that would help:

**When you get the 403 and then refresh and it works — is it always on the same page (e.g., the main dashboard view), or does it happen on different pages/actions?** For instance, does it only fail when you first load the dashboard, or also when navigating between sections, applying filters, etc.?

This will help us figure out whether this is a backend routing issue (e.g., load balancer hitting different servers with inconsistent config) versus a caching or session-timing problem.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It happens on pretty much any page, not just the dashboard. I've gotten the 403 on the main dashboard, on the reports page, and I think once on the settings page too. It's not tied to one specific action — it's like sometimes the whole app just rejects me. When I refresh it usually works again, sometimes I have to hit refresh a couple times. It does feel kind of random, like maybe 1 out of 3 times it fails? I hadn't thought about a load balancer thing but that's an interesting theory.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks — this is painting a pretty clear picture. Here's my working theory:

**The recent deployment that added the 'analyst' role wasn't applied consistently across all your backend server instances.** Some instances have the role properly configured, others don't recognize it — so when your request hits a "good" instance you get through fine, and when it hits a "bad" one you get a 403. That explains the ~1-in-3 failure rate and why refreshing works (you just get routed to a different instance).

Does that match what you're seeing? And one thing I still need to nail down: **next time you get a 403, can you open the browser Network tab, click the failed request, and share the response headers?** Specifically I'm looking for a `Server` header (e.g., `nginx`, `cloudflare`, `gunicorn`) and any `X-Request-Id` or `X-Served-By` header. This will tell us whether the 403 is coming from your application or from a proxy/CDN layer in front of it — which changes where the fix needs to happen.

If you have access to your ops team, it might also be worth asking them to check whether all instances are running the same deployment version.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah, that theory actually makes a lot of sense — way more than the 'network issues' I was blaming it on. The 1-in-3 thing really does match up with what I've been experiencing. I'll try to grab those headers next time it happens, though I'm not super familiar with the Network tab so bear with me. I do know we run nginx but that's about the extent of my infrastructure knowledge. I'll ping our ops team and ask them to check if all the servers got the latest deployment — I think we have 3 servers behind the load balancer if I remember right. I'll update you once I hear back from them or once I catch the headers.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent 403 errors for users with 'analyst' role due to likely inconsistent deployment across backend instances","problem":"Users assigned the newly-added 'analyst' role receive 403 Forbidden errors on approximately 1 in 3 page loads across all application pages (dashboard, reports, settings). Refreshing one or more times resolves the error temporarily. Multiple team members with the analyst role are affected. The issue began shortly after a deployment that introduced the analyst role.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The deployment that added the 'analyst' role was not applied consistently across all 3 backend server instances behind the load balancer. Instances that received the update recognize the analyst role and grant access; instances that did not receive (or fully apply) the update do not recognize the role and return 403. Round-robin or similar load balancing distributes requests across all instances, producing the intermittent ~1-in-3 failure pattern.","reproduction_steps":["Log in as a user with the 'analyst' role","Navigate to any page (dashboard, reports, or settings)","Observe that approximately 1 in 3 page loads returns a 403 Forbidden","Refresh the page — it will usually succeed on retry (routed to a different instance)"],"environment":"TaskFlow web application behind nginx with 3 backend server instances and a load balancer. A recent deployment introduced a new 'analyst' role. Standard (non-custom) 403 error page is returned.","severity":"high","impact":"All users with the 'analyst' role are intermittently locked out of the entire application. This appears to affect an entire team that was recently migrated to the new role. Users can work around it by refreshing, but the experience is disruptive and unreliable.","recommended_fix":"1. Immediately verify the deployment version running on all 3 backend instances — confirm whether they are running the same version. 2. If version skew is found, redeploy the latest version to the out-of-date instance(s). 3. Review the analyst role's permission/authorization configuration to ensure it grants access to all standard application routes. 4. After redeployment, verify the fix by making multiple requests as an analyst-role user and confirming no 403s occur. 5. Investigate why the deployment was not applied to all instances (CI/CD pipeline issue, rolling deploy failure, etc.) to prevent recurrence.","proposed_test_case":"Authenticate as an analyst-role user and make 20+ sequential requests to various application endpoints (dashboard, reports, settings). Assert that all requests return 200 and none return 403. Optionally, pin requests to each backend instance individually and verify consistent 200 responses from all instances.","information_gaps":["Exact deployment versions running on each of the 3 backend instances (reporter is asking ops team)","Whether the 403 originates from the application layer or from nginx (response headers not yet captured)","Whether users with non-analyst roles (admin, editor) are definitively unaffected (reporter is checking with teammates)"]}
